body: The carbon budget is limited, and the agreement gives countries a common target and provides a means by which to collaborate on how to reach that target. Contrast that with: _"Let's have all countries do their own thing, and hopefully we'll get to the target which we haven't agreed to arrive at. And don't worry, climate change is only mankind's greatest self-imposed existential threat."_

body: >In the past we expected it to be their job. You bring up the example of global climate change. I am not a politician nor am I lobbied and I promise you I know more about this issue, hell even economically, than the average politician. No one pays me to be a smart person who knows things and yet I am. It doesn't sound like you really understand how busy our politicians are. They spend vast amounts of time actually working on legislation, spending time in committees, speaking with constituents, etc. They also have a large number of LAs on staff who's job it is to do all the fact finding and then summarize it for their boss so then when they go a meeting or whatever they are informed. But part of that fact finding process involves talking to people who are very familiar with an issue and those people are generally lobbyists. Indeed, lobbyists often build excellent working relationships with LAs because the information they provide is invaluable to the LAs job. I also want to make it clear, as a lobbyist the majority of your time will be spent with LAs. Oh by the way, I am not sure how familiar you are with Congressional staffers but an LA is a Legislative Assistant and avoiding gross consolidation even if it leaves a couple large competitors (telecom). The laws are not cosmetic. Once again, under Bush these laws were not enforced as vigorously as they had been in the past, that is beginning to change though it might be until the next president that we see a return to the old status quo. >We would need less tax revenue if we didn't spend in the billions to not only give tax cuts but also give subsidy to these very industries. First we don't spend anything giving companies tax cuts, second subsidies make up a very small portion of our annual budget. For instance in 2005 they spent $57 billion on subsidies to businesses of all kinds (though the largest portion goes to farmers) and in 2006 it was $95 billion.
label: climate policy critique
selection_reason: conf=0.990, margin=0.851, kw+sem agree on climate policy critique
